stem: An associate plans to publish an artifact to share a tool with people outside the company. The documented privacy caution is:
options: Publishing is always private, so anything is fine to include
Don't include confidential or sensitive information, since publishing/sharing makes it accessible to others
Published artifacts can never be viewed by anyone
Sharing requires each recipient to buy a license
rationale: Publishing or sharing an artifact makes it accessible to others (recipients may not even need an account), so you should avoid including confidential or sensitive information. For AI-powered artifacts, consider whether shared storage is used before entering sensitive data.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — What are artifacts and how do I use them

stem: A team handling confidential client work wants persistent context per client but no cross-client leakage. Per the documentation, the safest structure is:
options: One global memory shared across all clients
A separate Project per client, since project memory and knowledge are scoped and don't bleed across projects or into global memory
One long incognito chat for everything
Putting all clients in a single project
rationale: Each project has its own scoped memory, knowledge base, and chat history that don't bleed into other projects or global memory — a documented containment guardrail well suited to per-client confidentiality.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Chat search and memory

stem: Before Claude uses connector data, the documentation says Claude accesses your Gmail/Drive/Calendar:
options: Continuously in the background
Only when you explicitly ask a question or request an action that requires it, retrieving the minimum needed
Only for Enterprise admins
By copying your entire mailbox on connect
rationale: Claude accesses connector data only when you explicitly ask something that requires it and retrieves the minimum information needed, consistent with least-privilege handling of your data.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Use Google Workspace connectors

stem: An Enterprise admin wants to remove Google Workspace connector access for the whole organization. Per the documentation, they can:
options: Not change it once enabled
Disable the connectors in Organization settings (Owners/Primary Owners control org-level connector access)
Only disable it per individual chat, never org-wide
Delete every user account
rationale: On Team and Enterprise plans, Owners and Primary Owners can disable Google Workspace connectors for the organization in Organization settings; individual users can also disable specific tools from the chat interface.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Use Google Workspace connectors

stem: An associate enabled a custom Skill but it never activates. Per the documentation, the most likely cause is:
options: The skill file is too short
The skill's description is too vague, so Claude doesn't recognize when to load it
Skills only work on Tuesdays
The account has too many projects
rationale: Skills load based on their description via progressive disclosure. A vague description is the common reason a skill 'doesn't fire'; tightening the description to clearly state when to use it fixes it.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — What are skills

stem: Claude isn't producing artifacts (or creating files) for an associate. The documented first thing to check is:
options: Whether it is a leap year
Whether Code execution and file creation is enabled in settings/capabilities
Whether the associate has five projects
Whether memory is turned off
rationale: Artifacts require Code execution and file creation to be enabled (Settings > Capabilities, or Organization settings for Team/Enterprise). Without it, artifacts are no longer supported.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — What are artifacts and how do I use them

stem: A Google connector stops working with a 'you may need to reconnect' error. The documented remedy is to:
options: Delete your Claude account
Go to Customize > Connectors, disconnect the Google connector, then re-authenticate on next use
Wait a year
Switch to incognito chat
rationale: The documented fix is to disconnect the Google connector under Customize > Connectors and re-authenticate the next time you use a Workspace feature; if issues persist, remove the Claude connection from your Google account settings.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Use Google Workspace connectors

stem: An associate's long single chat keeps losing track of earlier context and mixing topics. A documented, structural way to keep specialized guidance available without overloading context is to:
options: Paste all guidance into every message
Encode the recurring guidance as a Skill, which loads only when relevant via progressive disclosure
Turn off memory
Rename the chat
rationale: Skills load on demand through progressive disclosure, keeping specialized instructions available without cluttering the context window — a cleaner approach than pasting everything into every prompt.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — What are skills

stem: Anthropic's documentation gives a 'golden rule of clear prompting.' What is it?
options: Always make prompts as short as possible
Show your prompt to a colleague with minimal context; if they're confused, Claude likely will be too
Always include at least five examples
Never give Claude any context
rationale: The golden rule: show your prompt to a colleague who has minimal context and ask them to follow it. If they're confused, Claude probably will be too — a practical clarity check.
referenceText: Claude Docs — Be clear, direct, and detailed

stem: The docs suggest thinking of Claude as which of the following when writing prompts?
options: A mind reader who infers unstated intent
A brilliant but very new employee (with amnesia) who needs explicit instructions and context
A search engine that only needs keywords
A coworker who already knows your norms
rationale: Anthropic frames Claude as a brilliant but brand-new employee with amnesia: it lacks your norms and context, so the more precisely you explain what you want, the better the result.
referenceText: Claude Docs — Be clear, direct, and detailed

stem: Per the prompt-engineering guidance, the most effective way to convey a desired style or format is often to:
options: Stack many adjectives like 'nice' and 'professional'
Provide one good example of the desired output (a sample beats adjectives)
Ask Claude to guess the format
Avoid examples to save tokens
rationale: The docs note that one good example beats multiple adjectives describing what you want. Showing input-output samples (multishot) reliably steers format and style.
referenceText: Claude Docs — Prompt engineering best practices

stem: Which instruction reflects the documentation's advice to 'be specific'?
options: 'Be concise.'
'Write 5 bullets, each under 15 words.'
'Make it good.'
'Summarize this.'
rationale: The guidance favors explicit, measurable constraints ('5 bullets, each under 15 words') over vague directives like 'be concise,' which leave room for misinterpretation.
referenceText: Claude Docs — Prompt engineering best practices

stem: Per the docs, assigning Claude a role or expert persona (via a system prompt) primarily helps by:
options: Guaranteeing factual accuracy
Setting the appropriate perspective, tone, and vocabulary for the task
Removing the need to review output
Increasing the usage limit
rationale: Role prompting defines an expert persona so Claude adopts the right perspective, tone, and vocabulary. It shapes communication style, not factual guarantees.
referenceText: Claude Docs — Prompt engineering best practices

stem: The documentation's 'prompt chaining' technique means:
options: Sending the same prompt repeatedly
Breaking a complex task into a sequence of smaller prompts/steps
Chaining multiple accounts together
Removing all structure from a prompt
rationale: Prompt chaining breaks a complex task into ordered steps (for example, outline first, then expand each section), which improves reliability and lets you check intermediate results.
referenceText: Claude Docs — Prompt engineering best practices

stem: To reduce hallucinations, Anthropic's prompting guidance recommends explicitly:
options: Telling Claude to always be confident
Allowing Claude to say 'I don't know' when it is unsure
Forbidding Claude from citing sources
Asking for the longest possible answer
rationale: Explicitly giving Claude permission to say 'I don't know' (and to flag uncertainty) reduces fabricated answers — especially important for factual and RAG-style tasks.
referenceText: Claude Docs — Prompt engineering best practices

stem: According to the docs, which statement about prompt length is accurate?
options: Longer, more complex prompts are always better
Longer prompts are not always better; clarity and specificity matter more than length
Prompts should never include context
Only one-word prompts work well
rationale: The documentation warns against over-engineering: longer prompts aren't inherently better, and verbosity without added structure can make outputs worse. Start with clear, explicit instructions.
referenceText: Claude Docs — Prompt engineering best practices

stem: The prompt-engineering docs describe using XML tags or clear delimiters mainly to:
options: Make the prompt look technical
Separate instructions from data/context so Claude follows them reliably
Increase the model's speed
Bypass usage limits
rationale: Tags or delimiters clearly separate instructions, context, and examples so Claude can tell them apart; modern models also do well with clear headings and explicit language.
referenceText: Claude Docs — Prompt engineering best practices

stem: An associate refined a prompt for one model and worries it will break on the next model version. Per the docs, prompts:
options: Must be fully rewritten for every model update
Largely persist through model upgrades, unlike fine-tuned approaches
Stop working after any update
Are never affected by the model
rationale: Prompt engineering works atop existing models and prompts largely carry over through model upgrades, which is one advantage over fine-tuning.
referenceText: Claude Docs — Prompt engineering best practices

stem: Per the documentation, a good first step when you don't have a draft prompt is to:
options: Give up and use another tool
Use the prompt generator in the Claude Console to draft one
Write a 2,000-word prompt immediately
Copy someone else's password
rationale: The docs point to the Console's prompt generator to create a solid first-draft prompt when you're starting from scratch, then iterate from there.
referenceText: Claude Docs — Prompt engineering best practices

stem: Which are documented prompt-engineering techniques for Claude? (Select THREE.)
options: Be clear and direct with explicit instructions
Provide examples of the desired output
Give Claude a relevant role via a system prompt
Rely on vague, ambiguous wording
Never provide any context
rationale: Clarity/explicit instructions, examples (multishot), and role prompting are all documented techniques. Ambiguity and withholding context reduce quality.
referenceText: Claude Docs — Prompt engineering best practices

stem: The docs note that giving Claude more contextual information usually:
options: Confuses the model
Improves its performance, just as more context helps a person do a task
Has no effect
Is against policy
rationale: Just as a person performs better with more context, Claude performs better when given relevant background — purpose, audience, constraints, and examples.
referenceText: Claude Docs — Be clear, direct, and detailed

stem: For a task Claude only needs to do once, the documentation suggests:
options: Always build a Skill first
Just prompt well — explain what you want, give context, and refine
Never prompt; wait for a template
Create a new Project for every one-off task
rationale: For one-off or exploratory tasks, good prompting is enough. Skills are for approaches you want Claude to apply consistently across many conversations.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Skills

stem: A benefit of Claude's Research feature for validation, per the docs, is that it delivers reports with:
options: No sources, to keep them short
Easy-to-check citations so you can verify the findings
Only Claude's opinions
Randomized facts
rationale: Research produces thorough answers with easy-to-check citations, so you can trace and verify claims against the cited sources rather than trusting them blindly.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Use research on Claude

stem: Claude generates an Excel financial model via file creation. Before relying on it, the documented best practice is to:
options: Trust it since Claude wrote the formulas
Review the formulas, data, and any sensitive content — treat the first draft as a strong starting point
Delete it and redo it by hand
Publish it immediately
rationale: File-creation outputs are a strong starting point, but the guidance is to keep a human in the loop: verify formulas and data and review sensitive content before sharing.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Create and edit files with Claude

stem: An associate needs a polished, downloadable deliverable (a formatted report) rather than an on-screen answer. The documented capability is:
options: Incognito chat
Code execution and file creation, which produces real .docx/.pdf/.xlsx/.pptx files
The project name field
Turning off memory
rationale: Code execution and file creation generates actual downloadable Office/PDF files (with formatting, formulas, and charts), unlike an inline text answer or an in-app artifact preview.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Create and edit files with Claude

stem: Per the 'when to use' guidance, extended thinking is best suited to:
options: Simple lookups that need a fast answer
Complex reasoning where Claude benefits from planning its approach before answering
Reducing the length of every answer
Avoiding any tool use
rationale: Extended thinking suits complex reasoning tasks; it lets Claude plan its approach. It can also be combined with research to plan and then gather information comprehensively.

stem: The documentation says the Research feature's final output is:
options: A single short sentence
A longer, synthesized report with citations across multiple sources
Only a list of links
An audio file

referenceText: Claude Help Center — Web search, extended thinking, and research

stem: An associate wants Claude to turn a messy CSV into a clean analysis with charts and a written summary. The most fitting documented approach is:
options: Ask for advice on how to do it in Excel themselves
Use code execution / file creation, which can clean data, analyze it, and produce formatted outputs
Use incognito chat
Rename the file
rationale: Code execution lets Claude perform data cleaning, analysis, visualization, and Python-based data science, returning formatted deliverables — the right fit for turning raw data into insights.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Create and edit files with Claude

stem: When comparing outputs for an external audience, the documentation's editing feature for Markdown artifacts lets you:
options: Only regenerate the whole thing
Highlight specific text and use 'Edit with Claude' to change it in place
Edit only on Enterprise
Never edit once created

stem: Per the docs, when should an associate reach for web search rather than answering from Claude's own knowledge?
options: When the answer is a stable historical fact
When the request depends on current, changing, or recent information
Never — web search is not for facts
Only for creative writing
rationale: Web search is for information that is current, changing, or outside the model's training data. Stable, well-known facts don't require it.

stem: Which outputs warrant human verification before external use, consistent with the file-creation guidance? (Select TWO.)
options: An Excel model's formulas and figures
A financial report headed to a client
Nothing — Claude's files are always final
Only the file name
rationale: The documentation stresses reviewing formulas, data, and sensitive content in generated files. High-stakes, client-facing outputs especially need verification.

stem: Combining extended thinking with Research, per the docs, is useful because Claude can:
options: Skip gathering information
Plan its approach thoughtfully and then execute comprehensive, cited information gathering
Answer without any sources
Reduce the report to one line
rationale: The documentation notes that pairing extended thinking with research lets Claude both plan carefully and carry out thorough, multi-source information gathering — good for proposals and literature reviews.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Web search, extended thinking, and research

stem: An associate asks Claude to convert a Word report into a slide deck. Per the docs, file creation:
options: Cannot convert between formats
Can change a document from one format to another while preserving or enhancing the content
Only works for spreadsheets
Requires manual retyping
rationale: File creation supports cross-format conversion (for example, Word to PowerPoint) while preserving or enhancing content, so you don't rebuild the deliverable by hand.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Create and edit files with Claude

stem: Which best reflects the documented purpose of citations in Research outputs?

To let you check the sources behind each claim and trust the findings
To make the report longer
To hide the sources
rationale: Citations exist so findings are verifiable — you can follow each cited source to confirm the claim, which supports responsible evaluation of AI output.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Use research on Claude

stem: For a quick, single factual lookup during a task, the documentation indicates the best-fit tool is usually:
options: The full Research feature
A standard web search (a single search returning an answer)
Incognito chat
A new Project

stem: An associate is unsure whether an output is complete for a multi-part request. A sound documented habit is to:
options: Assume completeness if it's well formatted
Check the output against each part of the request and required sections
Only count the paragraphs
Publish and fix later

stem: When a single CSV is turned into a PowerPoint, PDF, and Excel workbook at once, the documented expectation for each file is that it:

Maintains professional formatting and opens directly in Office or Google Workspace
Can only be viewed in Claude
Is uneditable
rationale: The documentation highlights that coordinated multi-format outputs keep professional formatting and open directly in Office or Google Workspace, remaining fully editable.

stem: A responsible reviewer treats a Claude-generated first-draft document as:
options: The final, unreviewable version
A strong starting point to verify and refine before sharing
Proof no review is needed
Something to publish unread
rationale: The guidance frames generated files as a strong starting point; the associate still validates data, formulas, and sensitive content and refines before external use.

stem: Per the documentation, code execution and file creation (Excel/Word/PowerPoint/PDF) is available on:
options: Enterprise only
All plans — Free, Pro, Max, Team, and Enterprise
Pro only
No plans yet

stem: An associate turns on Research but it isn't investigating. Per the docs, Research requires:
options: Extended thinking to be off
Web search to be turned on
An Enterprise plan only
Incognito mode

stem: Per the 'when to use' guidance, Research is optimal for tasks that:
options: Need a single quick fact
Require comprehensive gathering across many sources (roughly five or more tool calls) and a synthesized report
Should avoid citations
Never touch the web
rationale: Research fits comprehensive information gathering requiring about five or more tool calls, synthesizing web and integration sources into an in-depth, cited report.

stem: According to the documentation, a plan's session-based usage limit resets:

Every five hours
Never
Every 24 hours only

Usage limits = how much you use Claude over time; length limits = how long a single conversation can get
Length limits control billing only
Usage limits apply to one chat

options: The same usage
At least five times the usage per session, plus priority access and early features
Less usage
Only API access

stem: The documentation states Team plans support:
options: Unlimited seats
Up to 150 seats (move to Enterprise for more)
Exactly 2 seats
One seat only

stem: Per the Team plan documentation, which is included with a Team plan?

Centralized admin and billing, SSO and role-based access, and connectors for workplace tools — with usage limits applied per member
API usage bundled into every seat
Unlimited seats with no administrative controls

Usage limits are per-member, not pooled. (Enterprise is for organizations that exceed Team's 150-seat cap or need further controls.)
referenceText: Claude Help Center — What is the Team plan

stem: Per the usage-credits guidance, to control token costs while keeping quality, associates should:
options: Always use Opus for everything
Use the efficient Haiku model or the most recent Sonnet for most tasks, reserving Opus for the hardest work
Never use Sonnet
Disable all models
rationale: The documentation recommends choosing efficient models — Haiku or the most recent Sonnet — for most tasks at lower token cost, keeping Opus for genuinely complex work.

stem: An associate keeps hitting a project's context limit. A documented way to maximize available context is to:

Use projects effectively (RAG), shorten project instructions, and remove unused files
Lengthen every instruction
Turn extended thinking on for everything

stem: Per the documentation, projects handle large amounts of information efficiently because they use:
options: A bigger model automatically
Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), loading only relevant content into context
Unlimited context on every plan
External email

stem: Which statement about memory availability matches the documentation?

Memory from chat history is available on all plans; searching past chats is paid-only
No plan has memory
Memory only works in incognito

stem: Per the documentation, incognito chat is available to:
options: Enterprise only
All Claude users
Free only
No one

stem: An associate wants deeper reasoning on a hard analytical task and is deciding on a toggle. The documented feature is:
options: Incognito
Extended thinking, which they can turn off when not needed to save usage
The project name
Web search only
rationale: Extended thinking provides enhanced reasoning for complex tasks; the docs also note turning it off when you don't need it helps optimize your usage.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — How usage and length limits work

stem: Per the documentation, usage credits let paid subscribers (Pro, Max):
options: Get unlimited free usage
Continue past included limits by billing further usage at standard API rates
Avoid all limits permanently
Downgrade the model

stem: After Claude creates a file, the documentation says you can:
options: Only view it in chat
Download it or save it directly to Google Drive
Only print it
Not access it

referenceText: Claude Help Center — Create and edit files with Claude

stem: Which are accurate per the file-creation documentation? (Select TWO.)
options: Claude runs code in a private, sandboxed computing environment
The maximum file size is 30MB per file
Files can only be created on Enterprise
Claude generates native video files
